**Part II: Learning to See**

# Becoming Wizards in the Woods

*Initiation*

---

In Washington State, in places I won't name precisely, there are educators who have decided that the most important thing they can do is put children in direct contact with old-growth forest.

Not teach them about old-growth forest. Not explain the carbon cycle or the spotted owl or the board-feet of timber at stake. Take them there. Let the forest do what it does to a child who stands inside it for long enough — the scale of it, the age of it, the specific quality of silence that isn't silence but is the sound of something much older and more patient than anything the child has encountered before.

I was one of them, for a summer. I want to tell you about the last day.

The group I was working with that week were five-year-olds, younger than I usually taught. The specialists in this age group had placed me there — selected me, they said, because they had seen a flash of gift in how I worked and intuited that I might be able to do the work they were doing. Five days in a state park, not at a designated camp, fully in the field. I'd built something workable. I didn't know if it was working.

On the last day, a deer walked through camp.

I didn't orchestrate this. Nobody did. The deer appeared at the edge of the clearing and the children saw it — and something happened that I had been trying to create conditions for all week without being able to force. They dropped. All of them, simultaneously, into a stalking gait — weight low, feet placed softly, the instinctive crouch of a predator or a prey animal moving carefully. And they went silent.

Do you know how hard it is to get a group of five-year-olds to be silent? For twenty minutes, we stalked that deer through the edge of the forest. The children moved like they'd been doing it all their lives, because in some evolutionary sense they had. Nobody told them to. Nobody needed to. The deer showed up and the children became, briefly, something older than themselves.

That's the whole thing. That's what the work is actually for.

The educators who do this work understand something that most policy advocates don't: that you can't protect what people can't imagine losing, and you can't imagine losing what you've never met. The intellectual argument for old-growth preservation is overwhelming. It hasn't been sufficient. The emotional and bodily argument — standing in the presence of a tree that was already old when your country was founded, breathing the air that the canopy produces — is different in kind, not degree.

I won't give you their names. They work on margins, and margins are vulnerable.

---

You recognized it. The deer at the clearing's edge, the children dropping, the silence — something in you knew the shape before any word could land. That is the wizard you are becoming. The book has been keeping the names for you. They are not new. They are yours, returned.

What the deer walked through, what the children moved on, what the educator had spent years coming to know — was the same thing. The forest. The state park's edges. The curriculum the educator had to rebuild for five-year-olds. The book calls this *the terrain*. The terrain is what the wizard reads. It is older than anyone walking on it; it produced them; they did not produce it. It has one fundamental axis: it has been enclosed, or it is held in common. The old-growth forest still stands at the commons end of that axis.

Why did the children need to be brought there at all? Because the world they had been born into had cast a small spell on them: the assumption that the important things happen indoors, on screens, between adults, far from anything alive. We call this *enchantment* — the way a specific perspective comes to feel like common sense. The mechanism is *incantation*: the slow spell by which one view of the world becomes the background everyone breathes. Incantation is not the tool of the powerful. It is what produces them, by producing the people who consent. The educator's craft is the counter-spell — a different incantation. *Take them there.* Maneuver the conditions. Trust the body to remember what the spell trained it to forget. The deer is the proof: the body had not forgotten. Every enchantment can be countered. Every spell can be re-cast.

The same spell that lays itself on the mind lays itself on the body. We call that *inscription*. The children had been carrying their bodies the way the world they were born into had taught them — eyes forward, breath shallow, feet placed for pavement. The deer asked the body for a different gait. The body remembered. Inscription too can be undone, given the conditions. The stalking gait was a counter-inscription. The flesh can be taught again.

What happened when the deer walked through camp was an example of *magic*. We'll see many more throughout this book. Magic is what happens when particular things meet and become, briefly, a single thing that was not there before. The deer carried *the secret ways* — the forest's irreducible particularity that no grid can map. Each child carried *the gift* — their singular angle on the world, the body they had inherited from generations whose names they don't know. I had taught them the stalking gait earlier that week. When the deer came, the body took over — and the body remembers things the mind has not yet caught up to. The body is what you have in common with your ancestors. When the deer appeared, what each child uniquely carried met what the deer carried and met what the other children carried, and for twenty minutes, the four of them — deer, children, morning, silence — became one thing. That is magic. It is the felt recognition of a composition coming together, like the climax of a symphony when the chord that has been gathering finally arrives and you feel it before you can name it.

Magic has kinds. The book will name them as you meet them — gathering and casting-out, hiding and revealing, rupturing the code, the dead acting and the not-yet-born acting, mastery felt and mastery measured. Each kind has both directions. Every spell can be cast for the commons or for enclosure — the same spell, two castings. There is never an unambiguous tool. The wizard's discipline is in the casting.

Every person has a *spellbook*. Not a printed book — a personal one, accumulated over years: the incantations they use to navigate the world. Each is different. The wizard who has done the work long enough has gathered the small techniques for loosening what binds, and for calling on what helps: when to speak and when to wait, which silences are productive and which are awkward, what acts on a five-year-old's attention and what releases it. Your first incantation is probably already in your mouth — a phrase you say all the time that isn't a catchphrase. Notice it. That's the first page. The spellbook is not secret. It is informal. It is yours, alone, to keep — to keep deliberately, to add to with intention. Anyone who decides to do this will find others doing the same. The networks exist. You follow the thread.

When you learn to read the terrain, to see the spells being cast, to cast your own and watch the magic work — what else can be said of you but that you are becoming a *wizard*? Someone who has walked the terrain long enough to recognize it when it rises again — to know, at the edge of a clearing, that what is happening must not be interrupted. There is no point at which one has fully *become* a wizard. There is only the becoming — the walking, the noticing, the reaching to be useful, ongoing. The educator was not a wizard. She was becoming one. Twenty years of practice, and she was still in it. The five-year-old who, sixty months into being alive, dropped into a stalking gait when the deer appeared — that child was also becoming one. They were at different points in the same becoming.

What happened in the forest that morning was small and complete — the children's first body-knowing of what the world is, the educator's twentieth or two-hundredth recognition of what she does, the reader's first sight of the work named. The book has a word for this too. *Initiation.* The walk of becoming a wizard. It changes what you can bear to see. It has no graduation. You were initiated once already — into the spell. This is the second initiation. The deer will walk through camp next summer. The educator will be there to not orchestrate it. The book ends; the work doesn't.

Notice the shape. These names are not a list. They are a working system. Yours to fill in.

*Terrain.* The field — enclosure ⇄ commons. *Incantation.* The slow spell traveling across it, cast on the mind. *Inscription.* The same spell, cut into the body — the gait, the gaze, the ambient knowing of which spaces are yours. *Naming.* The first move of any casting — the word that summons, binds, counters, or calls forth, depending on what is named. When you find the name, the body knows before the mind — an *oh* in the gut, the shoulders drop, the eyes open wider, the weight of unknowing lifts. *Magic.* What arrives when the work is done — singularities meeting in common. Its kinds will name themselves as you meet them. *Spellbook.* The list you are beginning. Every spell on it has both directions: the same incantation casts for the commons or for enclosure. There is never an unambiguous tool. The wizard's discipline is in the casting.

Read the terrain. Hear the spell. Feel the inscription. Name it true. Magic arrives. The spellbook fills.

And the book is a system too. Read it as a line, or walk it as a field — follow one force across the chapters: the pact, or the reading that reveals, or the road the birds route around, and watch it gather weight each time it returns.

There is an older terrain beneath all the others: the sacred. Mircea Eliade spent his life proving the disenchanted world was the strange one. For almost everyone who ever lived, he showed, the world was charged — a stone was a stone and also a door. And across cultures that never met, he found the same thing: the descent, and the container every old initiation built to hold it. What Plotkin walks people into, Eliade found in culture after culture. Proof that the door is not exotic. It is human. It can be opened again.

Hold the other half. The same man gave his gift to the Iron Guard — the nation made holy, the stranger made profane, the sacred cast for fascism. He never took it back. So remember him whole. The sacred is a terrain, and the spell that re-enchants is the spell that damns, depending on the hand that casts it. The commons is the discipline that tells them apart: the holy entered by many doors, never fused into one people's claim.

---

Here are a few spells, from my spellbook to yours. You won't be able to cast them — yet.

The first is *reading*. The terrain is what you read. When I'm in the field with a group, I'm paying attention to what everyone around me is paying attention to — and also to what they're not attending to. I'm listening to what people are saying and what they're not saying, watching how their bodies agree or disagree with their words. I'm listening to the birds, who are commenting on our collective energy and also on things I can't perceive yet. I'm feeling the moisture in the air, noticing how ambient sound and light shift, tracking how all of this affects the people around me. All of this at the same time. This is not exceptional — it's what human beings are built to do. We've just been trained out of it.

The second is *naming*. Every spell begins with a name. What I'm watching for is the moment when someone notices something they haven't noticed before and gets curious. It's like a seed sprouting. My job is to water it — but carefully. Too much attention, too much validation or explanation, and the seed drowns: the person returns to self-consciousness instead of opening further into environmental awareness. Too little, and the moment passes. The timing is everything, and it can't be forced.

The third is *building*. The part of spellcasting that doesn't speak — that moves the room instead. I'm not telling anyone anything when I'm guiding well. I'm maneuvering the conditions — the direction we're facing, the pace we're walking, what I say and what I leave unsaid — so that a connection can happen that I couldn't produce directly. When it works, I'm not sure I did anything. That's the sign it worked.

The fourth is *holding*. An invisible form of magic — the silence and stillness a spell needs to gather. There are moments when nothing is happening, and the urge to fill the silence is enormous — a child sitting with grief or confusion, a group whose energy has flattened, an afternoon when the deer does not come. The wizard's work in those moments is to hold. Not to fix. Not to explain. Not to introduce a new activity to break the stillness. The body that stays present when the educator has nothing to offer is doing the most important work of the day. The grief gets to be grief. The confusion gets to be confusion. The flat afternoon gets to be a flat afternoon. And in that allowance, something moves that the educator could not have made happen. The body learns the cost.

These four — read, name, build, hold — are spells too. Subjectivities the wizard has cast on themselves, deliberately, over years. The wizard's first spellbook is the one they make of themselves. Yours too.

---

The work is patient. It measures its effects in decades. It will not produce a policy victory next year. It may produce, twenty years from now, a generation of voters, organizers, parents, and officials who understand in their bodies what is at stake when an old-growth forest is auctioned for timber. That understanding, once embodied, is very hard to undo.

This is what building the commons looks like at its slowest and most durable. Not a rally, not a lawsuit, not an election. A child standing in a forest, learning for the first time what old means.

The first practice is small. Once today, when you walk into a room — workplace, store, train car — let your body register the room before your mind decides what to do. Notice what you already knew before words touched it. Try to name it, privately, silently, just for yourself. *That is what this is.* That naming is the wizard, beginning. Begin a private list. That list is the first page of your spellbook. You are crafting the wizard you will be.

Walk back into the world with the names. The next chapter is what the commons looks like when the work has had time to set, and to bear weight, and to be inherited. Eighty years' worth, in a mountain town in northern Spain.
